title: Chapter 2 Quiz
description: Test your mastery over C data types and memory!

questions.question: What happens when you declare a variable in C?
options: The computer prints it to the screen.
A box in memory is reserved for a specific type of data.
A new file is created on the hard drive.
questions.explanation: Declaring a variable tells the C compiler to reserve a spot in RAM with the specified name and size.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid variable name in C?
questions.explanation: Variable names cannot start with a number, cannot contain spaces, and cannot contain hyphens. Underscores are allowed.

questions.question: In C, the variables `Age` and `age` are considered exactly the same.
questions.explanation: C is highly case-sensitive. `Age`, `age`, and `AGE` are three completely different variables.

questions.question: Which basic data type is used to store whole numbers?
questions.explanation: The `int` type stores integers (positive or negative whole numbers).

questions.question: To store a number with a decimal point like `3.14`, which types could you use?
questions.explanation: `float` and `double` are both floating-point types used for decimals.

questions.question: What is the difference between `float` and `double`?
options: float stores negatives, double does not.
double gives you exactly twice the decimal precision of a float.
double can hold text, float cannot.
float is 8 bytes, double is 4 bytes.
questions.explanation: `double` stands for double-precision. It uses 8 bytes (compared to float's 4) to store many more decimal places accurately.

questions.question: To declare a single character variable, you must surround the letter with _____ quotes.
questions.explanation: Characters use single quotes like `'A'`. Double quotes `"A"` are strictly for Strings.

questions.question: In the line `printf("Age: %d", age);`, what does the `%d` do?
options: It acts as a format specifier placeholder where the integer variable will be inserted.
It creates a new double variable.
questions.explanation: `%d` tells printf: 'Expect an integer variable here!'

questions.question: Which format specifier is used to print a `float`?
questions.explanation: `%f` is the format specifier for floating point numbers.

questions.question: C has a built-in `String` data type for handling text natively.
questions.explanation: C does not have a native String type! Strings are just handled as arrays of `char`.

questions.question: What does the `sizeof()` operator do?
options: Increases the size of a variable.
Returns the memory size (in bytes) of a data type.
Counts the number of letters in a string.
Measures the length of the code.
questions.explanation: `sizeof()` is used to ask the C compiler exactly how many bytes of RAM a specific data type consumes.

questions.question: On most modern 64-bit systems, how many bytes does a standard `int` consume?
questions.explanation: A standard integer usually takes up 4 bytes (32 bits) of memory.

questions.question: The `char` data type is the smallest type, predictably taking exactly _____ byte(s) of memory.
questions.explanation: A character is extremely lightweight and takes exactly 1 byte.

questions.question: What happens if you apply the `unsigned` modifier to an `int`?
options: It restricts the variable to only store positive numbers (and zero), doubling its maximum capacity.
It allows the variable to store decimals.
It makes the variable immune to viruses.
questions.explanation: By throwing away the ability to be negative, the binary space is entirely dedicated to counting upwards, doubling the positive limit.

questions.question: If you need to store the population of the entire world (around 8 billion), what type would you definitely need?
questions.explanation: A standard int maxes out around 2.14 billion. You would need an 8-byte `long long int` to hold 8 billion safely.

questions.question: What happens if you try to re-assign a new value to a variable declared with the `const` keyword?
options: The compiler throws a strict error and refuses to build the code.
The old value is quietly overwritten.
The program skips that line of code.
questions.explanation: The `const` (constant) keyword makes a variable strictly read-only after it is initialized.

questions.question: To create a Macro that pre-processes text replacements across your code, use the _____ directive.
questions.explanation: `#define` tells the preprocessor to find-and-replace specific terms before compilation even starts.

questions.question: A `#define` macro statement must end with a semicolon (`;`).
questions.explanation: Macros are pre-processor instructions, not standard C statements, so they typically do NOT end with a semicolon.

questions.question: What is the underlying reality of a `char` in C?
options: It is actually a tiny object-oriented class.
It is fundamentally an integer mapping to an ASCII code.
It is exactly the same as a Python string.
questions.explanation: Deep down, C sees chars as just small integers (usually from 0 to 255) that correspond to characters on the ASCII table.

questions.question: Which of these lines demonstrates BOTH declaration and initialization at the same time?
questions.explanation: `int age` guarantees the box. `= 25` puts the value in. Doing both on one line is called initialization.
